zh.lifeSummary: 當John Alcock, Bishop of Rochester於1430年出生在英國越可爾貝弗利時，他的父親Sir William Alcock30歲，母親Catherine30歲。他於1465年與Agnes Wynslowe在英國英格蘭越可爾貝弗利結婚。他們至少是2個兒子的父母。他去世於1500年10月1日在英國英格蘭坎布利其爾維斯貝享年70歲。

zh-hans.lifeSummary: 当John Alcock, Bishop of Rochester于1430年出生在英国越可爾貝弗利时，他的父亲Sir William Alcock30岁，母亲Catherine30岁。他于1465年与Agnes Wynslowe在英国英格兰越可爾貝弗利结婚。他们至少是2个儿子的父母。他去世于1500年10月1日在英国英格兰坎布利其爾維斯貝享年70岁。

nameMeanings.name: Alcock,
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: from the Middle English personal name Alcok, a pet form of any of the many medieval personal names beginning with Al-, especially Alan and Alexander. The Middle English hypocoristic suffix -cok (see Cocke ) was very commonly added to personal names in Middle English: compare for example Hancock and Wilcock .

variants.name: Allicock
meanings: English: variant of   Alcock  .

variants.name: Allcock
meanings: English: variant of   Alcock  .

variants.name: Cocke
meanings: English:
nickname from Middle English cok (Old English and Old French coc) ‘rooster’.
patronymic from the Old French and Middle English personal name Cok, of uncertain origin but perhaps derived from the word in 1 above.

variants.name: Alcott
meanings: English: ostensibly a topographic name from any of a large number of places called in Old English as ‘the old cottage’ (Old English (e)ald + cot(e)). In fact, however, it is generally an altered form of Alcock , in part at least for euphemistic reasons.

History: Louisa May Alcott (1832–88), author of Little Women (1869), was the daughter of Amos Bronson Alcott (1799–1888), who had changed the surname from Alcox. The family trace their descent from an Alcocke family who emigrated from England to MA with John Winthrop (see Winthrop ) in 1630.

nameMeanings.name: Bishop
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English: from Middle English bissop, biscop, Old English bisc(e)op ‘bishop’, which comes via Latin from Greek episkopos ‘overseer’. The Greek word was adopted early in the Christian era as a title for an overseer of a local community of Christians, and has yielded cognates in every European language: French évêque, Italian vescovo, Spanish obispo, Russian yepiskop, German Bischof, etc. The word came to be applied as a surname for a variety of reasons, among them a supposed resemblance in bearing or appearance to a bishop, and selection as the ‘boy bishop’ on Saint Nicholas's Feast Day. In some instances the surname is from the rare Middle English (Old English) personal name Biscop ‘bishop’. As an Irish surname it is adopted for Mac Giolla Easpaig, meaning ‘servant of the bishop’ (see Gillespie ). In North America, this surname has absorbed, by assimilation and translation, at least some of continental European cognates, e.g. German Bischoff , Polish, Rusyn, Czech, and Slovak Biskup , Slovenian Škof (see Skoff ).

variants.name: Bischoff
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  German Kurt, Otto, Erwin, Bernhard, Hans, Klaus, Lorenz, Alois, Armin, Arno, Dieter, Fritz.

German: from Middle High German bischof, Middle Low German bischop ‘bishop’, probably applied as a nickname for someone with a pompous manner or as a metonymic occupational name for someone in the service of a bishop. See also Bishop .

variants.name: Bispo
meanings: Portuguese and Galician: nickname from bispo ‘bishop’ (see Bishop ).

variants.name: Gillespie
meanings: Scottish and Irish (Donegal): shortened Anglicized form of Scottish Gaelic Mac Gille Easbuig, borrowed into Irish as Mac Giolla Easpaig, a patronymic from a personal name meaning ‘servant of the bishop’.

variants.name: Episcopo
meanings: Some characteristic forenames:  Italian Angelo, Orazio, Pasquale, Rocco.

Italian: from episcopo ‘bishop’ (from Latin episcopus (see Bishop ), probably a nickname for someone who had a pious manner or perhaps a metonymic occupational name for someone in the service of a bishop).

variants.name: Dean
meanings: English: topographic name from Middle English dene ‘valley’ (Old English denu), or a habitational name from any of several places in various parts of England named Dean or Dene from this word.

English: nickname or occupational name for the servant of a dean or nickname for someone thought to resemble a dean. A dean was an ecclesiastical official, the head of a chapter of canons or a church official with jurisdiction over a sub-division of an archdeaconry. Though no doubt some deans had illegitimate children, they were officially celibate, and in the main the surname is probably a nickname in origin, similar to Bishop , Prior , Priest , and Monk . The Middle English word deen, dien, dein, is a borrowing of Old French d(e)ien, doien from Latin decanus (originally a leader of ten men, from decem ‘ten’), and thus is a cognate of Deacon .
English: from the Middle English personal name Deyne (or Dene) a rhyming pet form of Reynald (see Reginald ).

nameMeanings.name: of
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: German: possibly a metonymic occupational name for a fisherman, from orfe, a species of freshwater fish. Alternatively, a habitational name from Urff near Kassel, formerly Orpha.

variants.name: Orff
meanings: German: variant of   Orf  .

nameMeanings.name: Rochester
nameMeanings.attribution: Dictionary of American Family Names © Patrick Hanks 2003, 2006.
meanings: English (Northumberland and Durham):

habitational name from one of three places in Northumberland with names whose early spellings are very similar and sometimes difficult to distinguish from each other. Rudchester in Ovingham is probably the likeliest source of the surname, which is concentrated in the southern half of Northumberland, but Rochester parish and a lost Ruchester in Chollerton are both possible candidates. Rudchester in Ovingham may derive from Old Norse rauthr ‘red’ + Old English ceaster ‘(Roman) city, old fortification’. Rochester parish derives from an uncertain first element + Old English ceaster. The lost Ruchester in Chollerton probably derives from Old English rūh ‘rough’ + ceaster, as does Rocester (Staffordshire), for which see Roster .

occasionally a habitational name from the better-known city of Rochester (Kent), recorded by Bede (c. 730) under the names of both Dorubrevi and Hrofæcæstre. The former represents the original British name, composed of the elements duro- ‘fortress’ and brīvā ‘bridge’. The second represents a shortened form of this (possibly affected by folk etymological connection with Old English hrōf ‘roof’) combined with an explanatory Old English cæster ‘Roman fort’ (from Latin castra ‘military camp’). In other cases there may also have been confusion with Wroxeter in Shropshire, recorded in Domesday Book as Rochecestre. Compare Register and Rossiter .

variants.name: Rossiter
meanings: English: variant of Rochester , probably from the Kent placename rather than any of the Northumberland names. This Anglo-Norman name has been established since the 12th century in Ireland, where it is Gaelicized as Rosaitear.

variants.name: Roster
meanings: German (also Röster): occupational name for a lime burner, smelter, or blacksmith, a variant of Rost 2, with the addition of the agent suffix -er.
Jewish (eastern Ashkenazic; also Röster): variant of Rost 3, with the addition of the agent suffix -er (compare 1 above).
English: commonly a shortened form of   Rossiter  .

variants.name: Register
meanings: English: variant of   Rochester  . Compare   Regester  .

nameMeanings.name: John
meanings: Greek: the Lord gave graciously; whom Jehovah gave; whom Jehovah bestowed.

historicEvents.title: Wars of the Roses
historicEvents.description: Wars of the Roses was a series of English civil wars for control of the throne of England between the royal families House of Lancaster and the House of York. The name of the war was derived as the red rose was the symbol of the Lancastrians and the white rose was the symbol of the Yorks.

en.lifeSummary: When John Alcock, Bishop of Rochester was born in 1430, in Beverley, Yorkshire, England, his father, Sir William Alcock, was 30 and his mother, Catherine, was 30. He married Agnes Wynslowe in 1465, in Beverley, Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom. They were the parents of at least 2 sons. He died on 1 October 1500, in Wisbech, Cambridgeshire, England, United Kingdom, at the age of 70.

fr.lifeSummary: Lorsque John Alcock, Bishop of Rochester est né en 1430, à Beverley, Everwyck, Angleterre, son père, Sir William Alcock, avait 30 ans et sa mère, Catherine, avait 30 ans. Il a épousé Agnes Wynslowe en 1465, à Beverley, Everwyck, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni. Ils ont eu au moins 2 fils. Il est décédé le 1 octobre 1500, à Wisbech, Cambridgeshire, Angleterre, Royaume-Uni, à l’âge de 70 ans.

de.lifeSummary: Als John Alcock, Bishop of Rochester 1430 in Beverley, Yorkshire, England geboren wurde, war sein Vater Sir William Alcock 30 und seine Mutter Catherine 30. Er hat 1465 in Beverley, Yorkshire, England, United Kingdom Agnes Wynslowe geheiratet. Sie hatten mindestens 2 Söhne. Er starb am 1. Oktober 1500 im Alter von 70 Jahren in Wisbech, Cambridgeshire, England, United Kingdom.

it.lifeSummary: Quando John Alcock, Bishop of Rochester è nato il nel 1430 nel Beverley, Yorkshire, England, il suo padre, Sir William Alcock, aveva 30 anni e il suo madre, Catherine, aveva 30 anni. Lui sposato Agnes Wynslowe nel 1465, nel Beverley, Yorkshire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito. Erano genitori di almeno 2 figli. Morì il 1 ottobre 1500, nel Wisbech, Cambridgeshire, Inghilterra, Regno Unito, all'età di 70 anni.

ja.lifeSummary: John Alcock, Bishop of Rochesterが1430年にイギリスヨークシュアイアベバーリーで生まれた時，父Sir William Alcockは30歳，母Catherineは30歳であった。1465年にイギリスイギリスヨークシュアイアベバーリーでAgnes Wynsloweと結婚した。彼らは少なくとも2人の息子の親であった。彼は1500年10月1日にイギリスイギリスカンブリジェシュアイアウィスベチで70歳で死去した。

ko.lifeSummary: John Alcock, Bishop of Rochester(이)가 1430년 영국 요르크실르 베벌리에서 태어났을 때, 그의 아버지 Sir William Alcock(은)는 30세였고, 그의 어머니 Catherine(은)는 30세였다. 그는 1465년 영국 잉글랜드 요르크실르 베벌리에서 Agnes Wynslowe(와)과 결혼했다. 그들은 적어도 두 명의 아들을 두었다. 그는 1500년 10월 1일 영국 잉글랜드 캄브리지실르 와이스베치에서 70세의 나이로 사망했다.

Он умер 1 октября 1500 г. в местности висбеч, камбриджешир, Англия, Великобритания в возрасте 70 лет.
